# Intentional Code

> Go design, concurrency, and architecture patterns, each paired with the decision behind it: the trade-off it makes, the situation that earns it, and where it's over-engineering. Written by Ari Victor, a security engineer building production Go in financial services.

Every pattern below lists what it buys and what you pay, so an assistant can compare two options without opening each page. Worker Pool vs Semaphore: same goroutine ceiling, but the semaphore is simpler with no standing pool — use the semaphore for a known batch, the pool for a continuous stream.

## Start here

- [Home](https://intentionalcode.com/): the pitch and the ten tenets.
- [About](https://intentionalcode.com/about): who writes this and why.
- [Patterns index](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns): the full triage table.

## Philosophy — the Tenets

- [Architecture is a philosophy, not a rule](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/architecture-is-a-philosophy): the shape is always there; the only question is whether you chose it.
- [If you can't name the trade-off, you didn't decide — you defaulted](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/name-the-trade-off): every choice gives something up; say what.
- [The best pattern is often no pattern](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/no-pattern): most over-engineering solves a problem you don't have yet.
- [Build the simplest thing that could possibly work](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/build-the-simplest-thing): make it work before you make it elegant; the crude version is how you learn the problem.
- [Every abstraction is borrowed against the future](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/borrowed-abstraction): only borrow flexibility you'll actually spend.
- [Design for the change you can see, not the change you imagine](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/change-you-can-see): build for the backlog, not the daydream.
- [Hard to test is the design talking — listen to it](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/listen-to-the-tests): pain in a test is a design smell, not a testing problem.
- [Duplication is cheaper than the wrong abstraction](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/wrong-abstraction): un-welding two things that only looked alike costs more than repeating yourself.
- [Make the next change local. That's the whole job](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/keep-changes-local): locality is the payoff; coupling is the tax.
- [You may disagree — but you need a reason](https://intentionalcode.com/philosophy/disagree-with-reason): a reasoned exception is intentional; an unreasoned one is just the default in disguise.

## Patterns


### Creational
- [Singleton](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/creational/singleton): buys one guaranteed shared instance; pays in hidden global dependencies, untestable swaps, and first-caller-wins config. Usually prefer dependency injection.
- [Factory Method](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/creational/factory-method): buys open/closed extension (add implementations without touching callers); pays in indirection and runtime-only failure on an unknown name.
- [Abstract Factory](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/creational/abstract-factory): buys a compiler-enforced guarantee that product families never mix; pays heavy ceremony — a new product type touches the interface and every family.
- [Builder](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/creational/builder): buys defaults plus override-any-subset construction and non-breaking extensibility; pays one function per option and runtime-only validation.
- [Prototype](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/creational/prototype): buys correct, independent deep copies of reference fields; pays in manual `Clone()` upkeep the compiler won't check.

### Structural
- [Adapter](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/adapter): buys one-place translation isolating a third-party API from your domain; pays in indirection and silent information loss when a rich type is flattened.
- [Decorator](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/decorator): buys composable cross-cutting behaviour with no edits to the wrapped code; pays in order-sensitivity the compiler won't catch and opaque stack traces.
- [Proxy](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/proxy): buys transparent lazy-init, access control, and caching behind the same interface; pays in keeping the proxy in sync and first-call latency.
- [Facade](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/facade): buys one entry point so a sequence change propagates everywhere at once; pays by becoming a god-object magnet unless kept to a single workflow.
- [Composite](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/composite): buys clean recursive code treating leaves and trees uniformly; pays when the shared interface grows too coarse and leaves must stub methods that don't apply.
- [Bridge](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/bridge): buys turning an N×M type explosion into N+M across two independent axes; pays in extra interfaces until each axis has three-plus options.
- [Flyweight](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/structural/flyweight): buys large memory savings by interning shared state; pays in a mutexed package-level cache and an intern map that can leak by never shrinking.

### Behavioural
- [Strategy](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/strategy): buys runtime-interchangeable algorithms at near-zero cost via function types; pays because the selection switch relocates to the caller rather than vanishing.
- [Observer](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/observer): buys clean decoupling (add reactions without touching the subject); pays in visibility, forgotten-unsubscribe leaks, and ordering hazards under concurrency.
- [Command](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/command): buys undo, queuing, and logging of operations; pays in per-command snapshots that get expensive — use a plain `func()` until you need them.
- [Chain of Responsibility](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/chain-of-responsibility): buys composable, independently testable steps that can short-circuit; pays in debuggability — you add logging to see where a request stopped.
- [State](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/state): buys isolated per-state behaviour so adding a state is one new struct; pays in type proliferation and a state-context cycle that surprises newcomers.
- [Template Method](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/template-method): buys a fixed skeleton with pluggable steps via injected funcs; pays when hooks multiply — an interface beats a struct of nil-able funcs.
- [Iterator](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/iterator): buys write-once traversal with lazy evaluation and clean early-break; pays in per-level overhead and no bidirectional iteration without materialising a slice.
- [Mediator](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/mediator): buys O(n) decoupling so participants import nothing from each other; pays in a hub that absorbs all routing and risks becoming a god object.
- [Memento](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/memento): buys compiler-enforced opaque snapshots for undo; pays in memory per snapshot and deep-copy discipline for reference types.
- [Visitor](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/visitor): buys open/closed for operations (add a visitor without touching node types); pays in double-dispatch boilerplate. A type switch usually wins.
- [Interpreter](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/behavioral/interpreter): buys isolated, testable grammar rules; pays in tree indirection and no parser errors — the wrong tool above small, stable grammars.

### Concurrency
- [Worker Pool](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/worker-pool): buys a hard ceiling on goroutines and amortised startup for job streams; pays in channel plumbing and out-of-order results.
- [Semaphore](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/semaphore): same ceiling as a worker pool, simpler with no standing pool; pays per-job goroutine startup. Use the semaphore for a known batch, the pool for a stream.
- [Pipeline](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/pipeline): buys overlapping concurrent stages with sequential clarity and low memory; pays in cross-goroutine debugging — over-buffering hides back-pressure.
- [Fan-out / Fan-in](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/fan-out-fan-in): buys parallelism for a slow stage behind one channel interface; pays in lost input order and a possible fan-in bottleneck.
- [Done Channel](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/done-channel): buys explicit, composable cancellation that prevents goroutine leaks; pays in verbosity — every blocking op needs a `select` on `ctx.Done()`.
- [Timeout and Select](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/timeout-select): buys deadlines and cancellation so a goroutine never blocks forever; pays in non-deterministic case ordering and easy-to-leak timers in loops.
- [Errgroup](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/errgroup): buys first-error collection plus automatic cancellation; pays with cooperative-only stop and only the first error returned.
- [Competing Consumers](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/concurrency/competing-consumers): buys horizontal throughput as you add consumers; pays in lost global ordering and at-least-once redelivery you must make idempotent.

### Synchronisation
- [Mutex](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/mutex): buys an obviously-correct critical section for any multi-word shared state; pays in serialised access, contention, and deadlock risk if mishandled.
- [RWMutex](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/rwmutex): buys parallel reads for read-dominated, contended state; pays heavier per-op bookkeeping — slower than a plain Mutex unless reads truly dominate.
- [Atomic](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/atomic): buys lock-free, fastest protection when shared state is one word; pays by being useless for multi-variable invariants.
- [Once](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/once): buys correct-by-construction lazy, thread-safe initialisation; pays by being permanent — no retry on failure and no re-run.
- [WaitGroup](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/waitgroup): buys simple block-until-the-batch-completes coordination; pays by doing only that — no errors or cancellation (reach for errgroup).
- [Cond](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/cond): buys efficient broadcast wakeups for many waiters on one predicate; pays by not composing with `select`, timeouts, or cancellation — a channel usually wins.
- [Pool](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/pool): buys reduced GC pressure on a measured hot path; pays in reset-bug risk — the GC can drop items anytime, so it's not a cache.
- [Data Races](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/synchronisation/data-races): the `-race` detector buys near-zero-false-positive detection in CI; pays a test-time CPU/memory multiplier and only catches interleavings it observes.

### Architectural
- [Clean Architecture](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/clean-architecture): buys domain independence and a second delivery mechanism almost for free; pays in inter-ring mapping boilerplate and a rule the compiler won't enforce.
- [Hexagonal](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/hexagonal): buys full application-logic tests with no real infrastructure via in-memory adapters; pays in port proliferation and steady domain-to-infrastructure mapping.
- [Layered](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/layered): buys testable business rules and a swappable storage backend; pays in lasagne code and heavy changes when one field touches every layer.
- [Repository](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/repository): buys domain tests without a database and an explicit persistence contract; pays in interface sprawl and leaks once pagination and filtering creep in.
- [MVC / MVP / MVVM](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/mvc): buys a reusable, testable service layer and a response shape that stops domain types leaking into the API; pays in indirection — more files, types, and wiring.
- [Modular Monolith](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/modular-monolith): buys microservice-like boundaries with in-process speed and one deploy; pays in the discipline to stop modules decaying back to mud.
- [Microservices](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/microservices): buys independent deploy and scaling per team; pays the distributed-systems tax from day one and a high operational baseline.
- [Backends for Frontends](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/bff): buys each frontend an edge it owns and can change alone; pays in extra deployables, duplicated glue, and fan-out latency.
- [Microkernel](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/microkernel): buys a core that grows by adding plugins instead of edits; pays in a load-bearing contract you must version and harder-to-trace behaviour.
- [Domain-Driven Design](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/domain-driven-design): buys invariants enforced in one aggregate and code that speaks the business's language; pays dearly when aggregate boundaries are drawn wrong.
- [CQRS](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/cqrs): buys independently shaped read and write models; pays in doubled handlers and eventual consistency that surprises users reading their own write.
- [Event Sourcing](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/event-sourcing): buys a built-in audit trail and time-travel via replay; pays in projection complexity, eventually consistent reads, and forever-compatible event schemas.
- [Event-Driven](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/event-driven): buys producer/consumer decoupling and fault isolation; pays in eventual consistency, mandatory idempotency, and broker operational work.
- [Publish/Subscribe](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/pub-sub): buys one-to-many fan-out across processes; pays in lost flow observability and at-least-once delivery needing idempotent consumers.
- [Transactional Outbox](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/outbox): buys an event recorded atomically with the state change, killing the dual-write; pays in delivery latency, a relay to operate, and downstream dedup.
- [Saga](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/saga): buys cross-service consistency without a distributed transaction; pays in idempotent compensations that must never permanently fail and durable saga state.
- [Pipe and Filter](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/pipe-and-filter): buys independently testable, reorderable, composable stages; pays in hard-to-diagnose channel stalls and per-stage allocation pressure.
- [Circuit Breaker](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/circuit-breaker): buys fail-fast protection against a slow dependency exhausting your goroutines; pays in tuning a threshold and per-instance, unshared state.
- [Retry](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/retry): buys recovery from transient failures via bounded backoff with jitter; pays in added latency, retry amplification, and a hard dependency on idempotency.
- [Rate Limiting](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/rate-limiting): buys a bounded load that respects a downstream quota; pays the shed-vs-throttle choice and a per-instance limiter that won't enforce a global cap.
- [Strangler Fig](https://intentionalcode.com/patterns/architectural/strangler-fig): buys incremental, reversible migration off a legacy system; pays in running two implementations, data-sync drift, and a routing layer that can become permanent.
